---
title: "Verification Bandwidth and Context Adequacy"
chapter_id: "verification-bandwidth-and-context-adequacy"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "verification_bandwidth"
  - "vcm_public"
  - "spinoza"
  - "treellm"
  - "vcm_editable"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Source notes exist for all assigned sources, but claim-level source mapping remains incomplete."
  - "Only protocol schema/fixture validation is implemented; no contradiction-rate or adequacy benchmark has been run for this chapter."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# Verification Bandwidth and Context Adequacy

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `verification-bandwidth-and-context-adequacy` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `verification_bandwidth`, `vcm_public`, `spinoza`, `treellm`, `vcm_editable` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `verification_bandwidth`, `vcm_public`; supporting: `spinoza`, `treellm`; connector/recovery: `vcm_editable` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `verification_bandwidth`, `vcm_public`, `spinoza`, `treellm`, `vcm_editable`; raw cache: `verification_bandwidth`, `vcm_public`, `spinoza`, `treellm`; connector/recovery: `vcm_editable` |
| Test state | `context_adequacy_record.valid.json` passes protocol fixture validation; `AsiStackProofs.VerificationBandwidth` implements finite adequacy/admission separation and inadequate-context support blocking gates; contradiction-rate, distractor-resistance, and escalation-harness tests remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter treats verification bandwidth as a claim-control boundary, not as a measured model law. The repo validates a context adequacy record, but it has not run the proposed contradiction-rate experiment, measured attention interference, or formalized the two-body verification limit.

## Problem

Long context does not automatically provide long-range reasoning or enough verification capacity.

The central distinction is between **generation capacity** and **verification bandwidth**. A model may have enough context to mention two documents, summarize them, or weave them into plausible prose while still lacking enough active workspace to compare the relevant semantic units in detail. In this chapter, context is adequate only relative to a target claim, a set of semantic units, a verification task, and a declared risk level.

The layer receives context packets from VCM, claims from the reasoning layer, and risk labels from governance. It emits an adequacy state: sufficient for drafting, sufficient for local consistency checks, insufficient for verification, or escalated to tribunal/proof review. The output is not more prose. The output is a decision about whether the currently loaded context can support the kind of claim the system is about to make.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Adding tokens can increase distractors, attention interference, and unverified summary burden.

The `verification_bandwidth` source frames this as a two-body problem: rigorous comparison requires enough effective workspace to hold the compared units together. If one document, codebase, or specification dominates the active workspace, it can suppress the ability to check another. If consistency is checked only through a chain of local summaries, non-adjacent contradictions can survive by transitive decay.

RAG and long context are therefore necessary infrastructure, not proof. They help place material near the model, but they do not certify that the model jointly verified all relevant constraints. Tree-structured representation, VCM pages, and Spinoza-style claim ledgers are useful only if they expose what was compared, what was summarized, what was left as residual, and what claim class the packet is allowed to support.

## Core Claim

[verification-bandwidth-and-context-adequacy.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Context systems must distinguish generation capacity from verification bandwidth and declare when context is inadequate.

The claim remains at `argument` support. Verification Bandwidth supplies the generation-versus-verification distinction, semantic-unit comparison frame, transitive-decay risk, and proposed contradiction-rate test. VCM supplies adequacy/admission states and context packet vocabulary. Spinoza supplies claim-ledger consequences. TreeLLM supplies a representation route for explicit semantic units. Editable VCM remains a refinement source, not a local benchmark.

## Mechanism

The mechanism turns context loading into an explicit adequacy contract. A context packet should identify the semantic units it claims to cover, the target claim or task, the compression path used to fit them, the loss or residual warnings, and the verification mode that was actually applied.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Source units"] --> B["VCM packet"]
  B --> C["Semantic-unit inventory"]
  C --> D{"Joint verification possible?"}
  D -- "yes" --> E["Adequate for target claim"]
  D -- "no" --> F["Inadequate or summary-only"]
  F --> G["Escalate: retrieve, split, tribunal, proof, or human review"]
  E --> H["Claim ledger support remains bounded by declared mode"]
  G --> H
```

- Model verification as bounded by mutual information, representation quality, and attention budget.
- Require adequacy labels for context packets.
- Route high-risk claims to stronger verification instead of more context alone.

The practical rule is simple: if the system cannot name the semantic units that must be jointly checked, it cannot claim verification. If the units are too large for joint checking, the system may still draft, summarize, or propose a claim, but the claim ledger must mark the verification state as inadequate, summary-derived, or escalated.

## Interfaces

The interface for this chapter is a compact adequacy record that travels with the context packet and the claim it supports.

- VCM declares adequacy.
- Spinoza checks claims.
- Planning decides escalation when adequacy is insufficient.

Minimum fields:

- `target_claim_id`
- `semantic_units`
- `compression_path`
- `verification_mode`
- `adequacy_state`
- `residual_risks`
- `required_escalation`

Planning consumes the adequacy state before assigning review effort. VCM uses it to decide whether retrieval, summarization, or splitting is required. Spinoza-style claim ledgers use it to block support-state promotion when the context packet is adequate for drafting but not for verification.

## Invariants

- Adequacy is explicit.
- More context is not treated as proof.
- High-risk claims pay verification tax.

These invariants are deliberately weaker than a theorem about model cognition. The book does not claim to know a model's true internal workspace. It claims that a governed stack should expose the boundary conditions under which a claim was produced, and should refuse to treat context length as evidence when the comparison task was not actually performed.

## Failure modes

- Attention interference.
- False confidence from large context.
- Verification skipped for cost reasons.

Three failures deserve special handling. First, a dominant source can crowd out a smaller but decisive contradiction. Second, a summary can preserve topic coverage while dropping the exact constraint that matters. Third, a chain of locally consistent summaries can conceal a global contradiction. Each failure should emit a residual record, not merely a lower confidence score.

## Minimal implementation

A context adequacy rubric and verification-bandwidth warning states.

The smallest useful implementation is deliberately narrow: a context adequacy rubric and warning-state schema. A toy fixture can give the system two or three documents with planted cross-document contradictions, then compare ordinary long-context drafting against a final explicit pairwise checking pass. The expected artifact is not a performance claim; it is a contradiction ledger showing which claims were checked, missed, escalated, or left unsupported.

The existing artifact is narrower: a `context_adequacy_record` schema and fixture. It records semantic units, compression path, verification mode, adequacy state, residual risks, and escalation requirements, but it does not measure verification performance.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Context adequacy fixture validation | Check that the adequacy fixture matches the public schema and declares target claim, semantic units, compression path, verification mode, adequacy state, residual risks, and escalation requirements. | implemented by protocol validation; run in this pass |
| Distractor resistance test | Compare contradiction detection when a decisive small unit is paired with a dominant distractor unit. | planned; not run |
| Adequacy labeling test | Check that drafting-adequate context is blocked from verification-backed support when pairwise checking is absent. | planned; not run |
| Verification escalation test | Check that high-risk claims with inadequate context route to tribunal, proof, retrieval, split-context, or human review instead of promotion. | planned; not run |

Only the fixture-shape check is implemented. The other tests require a synthetic contradiction suite, logged prompts or deterministic checkers, and claim-ledger outcomes.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:verification_bandwidth.adequacy.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.VerificationBandwidth` | A context packet admitted for use may still be marked inadequate for a target claim. | implemented |
| `lean:verification_bandwidth.adequacy.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.VerificationBandwidth` | A high-risk claim with inadequate context cannot receive a verified support label. | implemented |

These Lean hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates over context-packet and claim-support reviews. They prove only the record-level gates: admission and adequacy are distinct states, and a high-risk claim with inadequate context cannot receive a verified support label. They do not prove a model's internal verification bandwidth, contradiction-rate performance, distractor resistance, or escalation quality.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `verification_bandwidth` | Verification Bandwidth in Bounded Contexts | context_verification_theory | Generation vs verification; long context is not automatically long-range reasoning. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `vcm_public` | Virtual_Context_Memory_v1 | memory_context | Public VCM release. Governed protocol for compiled working context. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `spinoza` | Proof of Belief / The Spinoza Architecture | reasoning_epistemology | Neurosymbolic belief, transparent axiomatic AI belief systems, verification, belief revision. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `treellm` | TreeLLM | semantic_representation | Hierarchical semantic token system for grounded, efficient, explainable language modeling. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `vcm_editable` | Virtual_Context_Memory_v1.0_Editable | memory_context | Editable version with evidence-carrying planner-guided context compiler framing. | source note available; connector or recovery required |

The crosswalk identifies sources for context adequacy and verification-capacity vocabulary. It does not claim the proposed contradiction-rate experiment has been run.

## Summary

Verification Bandwidth and Context Adequacy defines one boundary in the ASI Stack: Context systems must distinguish generation capacity from verification bandwidth and declare when context is inadequate. Its job is to make a capability more governable, not merely more impressive.

The design principle is conservative: context can be present, relevant, and still inadequate for the claim being made. A living ASI stack should therefore record adequacy as a first-class state, route high-risk claims to stronger verification, and treat missed contradictions as residuals that improve the next context compiler.
